text: While ultrafine tuning the structure of MOFs at the subangstrom or even picometer level has the potential to enhance the selectivity between closely spaced gas components, the effect is often reduced as the impact of such fine adjustments to the structure is averaged out, particularly for MOFs with large enough pore diameters to accommodate the gas molecules. Here, we use an integrative strategy that compresses large pores to an extreme minimum by one of pore size and also fine-tunes their structure. Our ACS Materials Science & Engineering article entitled, Extreme pore compression of a MOF coupled with ultrafine structural adjustment shows that the unique features of the partitioned aromatic constraining scaffold (pacs) architecture (multimodular framework and its exceptional stability to isoreticular replacement) enabled using one ligand to achieve an extreme compression of the pore diameter by about 30% (based on the unit cell c/a ratio) from the prototypical 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate anchored on a pacs to that anchored on a trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate pacs framework. This is followed by adjusting the structure further at the subangstrom or picometer level by using another ligand. This unique dual-ligand modularity enables our L1-L2 strategy to increase the selectivity between two gas molecules that have minor differences, here illustrated by a 8 times increase in the C2H2/CO2 adsorption selectivity compared to the 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate anchored pacs from 2.6 to 20.8 while achieving an excellent experimentally measured breakthrough uptake. Interestingly, using the shortest cyclic dicarboxylate (which mimics the bioisosteric, p-benzene based moieties) anchored on a pacs and the BIS design concept affords opportunities for the development of new MOF architectures.
